back_translation: These are the nations the LORD let remain to test all the descendants of Israel — those who had not known any of the wars in Canaan,
so that the subsequent generations of the descendants of Israel might learn warfare — especially those who had not known it before:
the five rulers of the Philistines, all the Canaanites, the Sidonians, and the Hivites who lived in the mountains of Lebanon, from Mount Baal-hermon to the entrance of Hamath.
These nations were left to test Israel, to know whether they would obey the commandments of the LORD which He had commanded their fathers through Moses.
The descendants of Israel lived among the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites.
They took the daughters of those peoples as wives, gave their own daughters to the sons of those peoples, and served their gods.
The descendants of Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD; they forgot the LORD their God and served the Baals and the Asherah poles.
The anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and He sold them into the hand of Cushan-rishathaim king of Aram-naharaim. The descendants of Israel served Cushan-rishathaim for eight years.
When the descendants of Israel cried out to the LORD, the LORD raised up a deliverer for them — Othniel son of Kenaz, the younger brother of Caleb — who saved them.
The Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he judged Israel. He went out to war, and the LORD delivered Cushan-rishathaim king of Aram into his hand, and his hand prevailed over Cushan-rishathaim.
The land had rest from war for forty years, until Othniel son of Kenaz died.
The descendants of Israel again did evil in the sight of the LORD, so He strengthened Eglon king of Moab to have power over Israel, because they had done evil in the sight of the LORD.
Eglon gathered to himself the descendants of Ammon and Amalek, and went and struck Israel, and they took possession of the City of Palms.
The descendants of Israel served Eglon king of Moab for eighteen years.

The descendants of Israel cried out to the LORD again, and the LORD raised up for them a deliverer — Ehud son of Gera, a Benjamite, a left-handed man. The descendants of Israel sent him with tribute to Eglon king of Moab.
Ehud made for himself a double-edged sword about one gomed long (about half a meter), and strapped it to his right thigh under his cloak.
He brought the tribute to Eglon king of Moab; and Eglon was a very fat man.
When he had finished presenting the tribute, he dismissed the people who had carried it.
But he himself turned back from the idols near Gilgal and said, 'I have a secret message for you, O king.' The king said, 'Silence,' and all those standing with him left him.
Ehud came to the king as he was sitting alone in the cool upper room, and Ehud said, 'I have a message from God for you.' The king rose up from his throne.
Ehud reached out his left hand, drew the sword from his right thigh, and thrust it into Eglon's belly.
The handle also went in after the blade, and the fat closed over the blade, because he did not pull the sword out of his belly; and the dung came out.
Ehud went out through the porch, shut the doors of the upper room behind him, and locked them.
When he had gone out, the king's servants came and saw that the doors of the upper room were locked, and they said, 'He must be relieving himself in the cool room.'
They waited until they became embarrassed, and behold, he had not opened the doors of the upper room. So they took the key and opened them, and behold, their lord lay dead on the floor.
Ehud had escaped while they delayed. He passed by the idols and escaped to Seirah.
When he arrived, he blew the ram's horn in the hill country of Ephraim, and the descendants of Israel went down with him from the hill country, and he led them.

He said to them, 'Follow me, for the LORD has delivered your enemies the Moabites into your hand.' They followed him down and seized the fords of the Jordan against the Moabites, and they did not let anyone cross over.
At that time they struck down about ten thousand Moabites — all robust and valiant men — and not one escaped.
So Moab was subdued that day under the hand of Israel, and the land had rest from war for eighty years.
After Ehud came Shamgar son of Anath. He also saved Israel by striking down six hundred Philistines with an oxgoad.
